coredata.dc:title: 4-aminoantipyrine modified carbon dots and their analytical applications through response surface methodology
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Jiang, Yingxia
Sun, Xiaodong
Ma, Tengchuang
Chen, Liang
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and selective nanoprobe for detection of hypochlorite (OCl–) based on 4-aminoantipyrine (AAP) modified carbon dots (CDs-AAP) has been prepared. The CDs-AAP exhibit an emission peak at 484 nm when the excitation wavelength is 370 nm, accompanying 36 nm red shift compare with the pristine CDs. The addition of OCl– lead to the AAP on the surface of CDs experience a process of hydrazide hydrolysis and double bond addition, causing the singlet and triplet electrons of the excited state more closer in energy (ie, the energy difference between the two is reduced), eventually quenching the fluorescence of CDs due to heavy atomic effects. Central composite design (CCD) and response surface method (RSM) were used to optimize the detection variables of pH, incubation time and temperature. The designed model study indicated that the optimum detection conditions was pH 7.0, temperature 30 °C and incubation time 20 min, respectively. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent intensity of the nanoprobe linearly responded to the OCl– concentration from 3 μM to 36 μM and the limit of detection was 40 nM. The proposed nanoprobe was successfully used to the detection of OCl– in tap water and pool water, and the recovery were in the range of 94% – 103%. In addition, the nanoprobe was also applied in imaging of VMSCs cells and labeling E.coli.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Modified
Central composite design
Response surface method
Bioimaging

Fluorescence response of CDs-AAP to OCl–
Labeling of E. Coli and VMSCs cells
Optical properties of CDs and CDs-AAP
Fluorescence detection of CDs-AAP to OCl–
Application of OCl– in real water sample
Application of OCl– in cells labeling and imaging

Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A) and CDs-AAP (D), HRTEM images of CDs (B) and CDs-AAP (E); Size distribution histograms of CDs (C) and CDs-AAP (F).
(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs, AAP and CDs-AAP; (B) XPS analysis of CDs and CDs-AAP, the inset was the pie chart of elemental contents.
(A) Raman spectra of CDs and CDs-AAP; (B) Zata potential of CDs and CDs-AAP.
Tyndall effect image of CDs (left), small polarity of CDs-AAP (middle) and larger polarity of CDs-AAP (right).
(A) FL spectra of CDs under varied excitation wavelengths; (B) The changes of FL emission peaks of CDs under varied excitation wavelengths. The inset is fluorescence images of CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of CDs, AAP and CDs-AAP; (B) FL spectra of CDs-AAP under varied excitation wavelengths. The inset is FL images of CDs (left) and CDs-AAP (right).
Pictures of (A) Predicted vs. Actual values (B) normal probability plot of residuals (C) run number vs. internally. studentized residual.
Response surfaces for the central composite designs: (A) Temperature-pH (B) temperature-Time (C) Time-pH.

(A) FL spectra of CDs-AAP upon the addition of various OCl– under excited at 370 nm; The inset is the FL response versus the concentration of OCl–; (B) FL response of CDs-AAP to various anon without and with OCl–; (C) The color photographs of CDs-AAP to various anions under excited at 365 nm.

(A) XPS spectra of CDs-AAP upon addition of 40 μM OCl–; The inset is the XPS spectra of Cl2p; (B) FTIR spectra of CDs-AAP upon addition of 40 μM OCl–; (C) Schematic illustration of CDs-AAP for the detection of OCl−.
Viability of VMSCs cells after 48 h treatment with CDs-AAP as calculated from the MTT assay.

Upright fluorescent microscope images of CDs-AAP labeled E. coli at (A) bright-field; (B) blue channel; (C) green channel and Inverted fluorescence microscope images of VMSCs cells at (D) bright-field (E) blue channel; (F) green channel.
Comparison of different fluorescence probe for OCl− detection.

row.entry: tap water, pool water, E.coli, VMSCs cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of recovery and RSD studies of OCl− in tap water and pool water samples.

head.ce:title: 4-aminoantipyrine modified carbon dots and their analytical applications through response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo, 0010024, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo, 0010024, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and selective nanoprobe for detection of hypochlorite (OCl–) based on 4-aminoantipyrine (AAP) modified carbon dots (CDs-AAP) has been prepared. The CDs-AAP exhibit an emission peak at 484 nm when the excitation wavelength is 370 nm, accompanying 36 nm red shift compare with the pristine CDs. The addition of OCl– lead to the AAP on the surface of CDs experience a process of hydrazide hydrolysis and double bond addition, causing the singlet and triplet electrons of the excited state more closer in energy (ie, the energy difference between the two is reduced), eventually quenching the fluorescence of CDs due to heavy atomic effects. Central composite design (CCD) and response surface method (RSM) were used to optimize the detection variables of pH, incubation time and temperature. The designed model study indicated that the optimum detection conditions was pH 7.0, temperature 30 °C and incubation time 20 min, respectively. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent intensity of the nanoprobe linearly responded to the OCl– concentration from 3 μM to 36 μM and the limit of detection was 40 nM. The proposed nanoprobe was successfully used to the detection of OCl– in tap water and pool water, and the recovery were in the range of 94% – 103%. In addition, the nanoprobe was also applied in imaging of VMSCs cells and labeling E.coli.

Synthesis of CDs-AAP and detection of hypochlorite (OCl–) based on heavy atomic effect.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (OCl−), which is one type of the reactive oxygen species (ROS), can be used as a disinfectant and bactericide in the water environment and in the immune system of humans and animals, respectively [1–3]. Endogenous OCl– is a powerful oxidant with effective antibacterial activity during pathogen invasion. However, the excessive OCl– in vivo can lead to tissue damage and lead to many diseases including cardiovascular disease, inflammatory disease, neurodegenerative disorders and even cancers [4–6]. Conventional approaches have been used to detect OCl– including electrochemical analysis, chromatographic analysis, potentiometry, chemiluminescence [7–9]. Compared with the traditional methods, fluorescent probes have become one of the most ideal tools for detecting trace amounts of biological species in living systems because of their excellent specificity and sensitivity, superb spatiotemporal resolution and simple operation and real-time detection.

Fluorescence-based methods have attracted great interest in practical applications. Among them, the nanomaterial carbon dots (CDs) have more advantages in term of their low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility, selectivity and sensitivity [10–12]. Various of fluorescent probes based on CDs, in recent years, have been developed rapidly for bioimaging, sensing and drug delivery. Nevertheless, most of the researchers have focused on one-step synthesis of CDs with tunable optical properties and sensing capabilities. The CDs are obtained by this method usually without targeting selectivity, because the surface is short of specific interactions with target analytes [13–15]. To circumvent this problem, surface modification is a critical issue in CDs research to stabilize and expand their applications as required [16–20]. Chemical modification of the pristine CDs surface with organic molecules or fluorophores is an effective method, can be called as an amplification function principle of surface modification, for preparation of multi-functional CDs. Recently, some surface modified CDs have been successfully applied to analyte detection, cells imaging and drug delivery [21,22]. Wu et al. using the morpho-line group (ML), which is the targeting unit for lysosomes, synthesized ML-modified PEI wrapped CDs for specific labeling and imaging of lysosomes [23]. The modification promoted CDs-ML has improved water solubility and quantum yield. Especially, the biocompatibility of CDs and the targeting of ML provide ideas for synthesizing surface-modified CDs with required functionality.

In this work, carboxyl-rich CDs were synthesized with anhydrous citric acid as carbon precursors whose intrinsic reactivity can be directly used for further modification of their surface without any need of pre-activation. Then, the obtained CDs were covalently coupled with AAP through amide coupling reaction. The successful modification of CDs with AAP was investigated by TEM, UV–vis, FT-IR and Raman spectra. CDs-AAP was used to detect OCl−, in which AAP was chosen as the surface recognition group due to the hydrazide bond can be hydrolysis and open loop upon addition of OCl−, then the unsaturated double bond next to the amide bond undergoes a process of OCl− addition. Consequently, the fluorescence of CDs-AAP was quenched because of the heavy atomic effects. Since different detection conditions affect the sensitivity of CDs-AAP, the traditional method of “changing one factor at a time” is cumbersome and does not take into account the effects of interactions between variables. Therefore, RSM was used to optimize the detection conditions of CDs-AAP, including individual factors and the effects of interactions between them. Statistical results were provided by analysis of variance (ANOVA) to assess the significance of the model. In addition, CDs-AAP were applied for detection of OCl− in real water samples including tap water and pool water. The excellent physicochemical properties make CDs-AAP were also useful for labeling E. coli and imaging of VMSCs cells.

Anhydrous citric acid, 1-Ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl]carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 4-Aminoantipyrine (AAP) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All chemicals and reagents were of AR grade and used as received. All experiments were carried out using Milli-Q water.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a TENSOR37 IR spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Purkinje, General) and a F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Zata potential measurements were carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle size and zeta potential analyzer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a high-resolution JEOL 2010F TEM equipped with a high-angle annular dark-field detector. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Upright fluorescent microscope images were obtained on Olympus bx 53. Cells imaging were carried out on Nikon TE2000-U inverted fluorescence microscope (Japan).

Briefly, anhydrous citric acid (1.73 g, 7 mmol) and ethylenediamine (200 μL, 2.33 mmol) was mixed in distilled water (30 mL) with stirring until complete dissolution. The mixture was then transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling the reactor down to room temperature naturally, the solution was filtrated through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove large particles and dialyzed against a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted small molecules. The brownish yellow solid CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation under vacuum (1.21 g, 64.7%).

The conjugation procedure was carried out using standard EDC/NHS coupling reaction. In brief, 0.4 g CDs were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and stirred until dissolved, followed by addition of 5 mL of EDC·HCl (0.38 g, 2 mmol) solution, the reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min to activated carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. After that, 5 mL of NHS (0.12 g, 1 mmol) solution and 10 mL AAP (0.41 g, 2 mmol) solution were added to the mixture. The reaction was carried out at room temperature and protected from light for 48 h. Finally, two different polar CDs-AAP (CDs-AAP(S) and CDs-AAP (L)) were obtained by silica gel column chromatography, which may be attribute to the different amount of AAP was conjugated on the CDs surface. and we have proved this guess in the following text. The purified CDs-AAP were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C before further usage.

The quantum yield (Φ) of CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (literature Φ was 0.54) as reference. Firstly, CDs were dissolved in water while quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 to form 0.1 μg mol−1 solution. Then five concentration gradients of CDs and quinine sulfate were made. To minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbance in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.05 for quinine sulfate and 0.1 for CDs. The integrated fluorescence intensity is the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum at the optimal excitation wavelength. Then the graph was plotted using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance and the intercept is zero. The Φx was calculate as follow equation:
(1)
Φ
x
=
Φ
s t
G r a
d
x
G r a
d
s t
(
ŋ
x
ŋ
s t
)
2
where Φ is the quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance, η is refractive index of the solvent, and A is optical density. The subscript “st” refer to quinine sulfate and “x” refer to the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation the amount of AAP conjugated to CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The amount of AAP conjugated to per gram of CDs were calculated depend on the additivity of absorbance of CDs and AAP [24,25]. CDs display the characteristic absorption at 350 nm. AAP has two characteristic absorption peaks at 245 nm and 275 nm, which may change the conjugated structure due to modification with CDs, showing an obvious characteristic absorption at 275 nm. Therefore, 275 nm was used as the characteristic absorption of AAP. And the amount of AAP can be determined according the equations as follow:
(2)
A
275 n m
=
ε
C D s 275
×
C
C D s
× b +
ε
A A P 275
×
C
A A P
× b
(3)
A
350 n m
=
ε
C D s 350
×
C
C D s
× b +
ε
A A P 350
×
C
A A P
× b
In this equation, A is the absorbance of the samples at different wavelengths; ε is the absorptivity and C is the concentration; b denotes 1 cm optical length. εCDs275, εCDs350, εAAP275 and εAAP350 were acquired through measuring the absorption of CDs and AAP solution at different concentration. By measuring the absorption of the CDs at different concentrations and the absorption of AAP solution at different concentrations, εCDs275, εCDs350, εAAP275 and εAAP350 can be obtained. Then, the concentrations of CDs and AAP can be determined which in turn obtained the amount of AAP on per gram of CDs by using the above equation.

The CCD and RSM were used to estimate the effects of operating variables such as pH, incubation time and temperature on the fluorescence response (F0–F). Experimental factors and levels used in the CCD as shown in Table S1. The quadratic equation model used to predict the optimal detection conditions of the three independent variables can be expressed as follow Eq:
(4)
Y =
β
0
+
∑
i = 1
k
β
i
X
i
+
∑
i = 1
k
∑
j = j + 1
k
β
i j
X
i
X
j
+
∑
i = 1
k
β
i i
X
i
2
Where Xi and Xj is the detection variables;
β
0
indicate the constant for the model;
β
i
,
β
i j
,
β
i i
represent the linear coefficient, interactive effect coefficient and quadratic effect coefficient, respectively. The interaction between the detection variables of pH, incubation time, temperature and fluorescence responses were checked by analysis of variance. The quality of the fitted polynomial model was represented by the coefficient of determination R2, and its statistical significance was examined by the Fisher’s statistical analysis. Based on the effect of the interaction between the three factors on the fluorescence response, three-dimensional maps were obtained, which can determine the optimum detection parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs-AAP to OCl–
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of OCl– was performed at room temperature in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0). In a typical run, the stock solutions of CDs-AAP 2 mg mL−1 was prepared with 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer. All the test solutions were prepared by the following procedure. 100 μL of CDs-AAP stock solution was directly added to a 10 mL volumetric flask using a micropipette, then different volumes of OCl– stock solutions (3, 6, 9, 12 … 36 μL) were added and made up to 10 mL with Tris-HCl buffer. The resulting solution was shaken well and incubated for 2 h, and then the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded using 370 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensities measurements were conducted in triplicate. For comparison, the fluorescence response of CDs-AAP to other inorganic anions with the concentration of 2 mg mL−1 was also performed by the same method. All of these experiments were conducted at room temperature, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and the excitation and emission slits were set as 2.5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labeling of E. Coli and VMSCs cells
ce:section.ce:para: Labeling of E. coli and VMSCs cells by the CDs-AAP was carried out mostly according to our previous work [26]. First, E. coli and VMSCs cells were pre-cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin streptomycin mixture solution at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 1.5 h. Before cells imaging, the cells were washed thrice with PBS buffer, fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde and then mounted using 50% glycerol. The CDs-AAP labeled E. coli and VMSCs cells images were measured by Nikon TE2000-U inverted fluorescence microscope.

VMSCs cells were seeded into a 96-well plate for 24 h in the standard medium (37 °C with 5% CO2) [27]. Then CDs-AAP with different concentrations were added. After incubation for 48 h, the relative viabilities of the cell samples were obtained through colorimetric 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays to assess the cell alive. Finally, VMSCs cells were lysed with DMSO. The absorbance of MTT at 570 nm was measured on a microplate reader.

Inspired by some organic fluorescent nanoprobes for detecting OCl– based on the mechanism of oxidization hydrolysis of hydrazide, AAP with a hydrazide group was selected as a targeting molecule for selective identification of OCl–. The UV response of OCl– to AAP was studied as shown in Fig. S1, as the concentration of OCl– increased from 0 to 50 μM, the characteristic absorption peaks of AAP at 240 nm and 280 nm gradually decreased and red shifted, and a new characteristic absorption peak appeared at 340 nm, which was due to the OCl– hydrolyzed the hydrazide group in AAP and generate the auxochromic groups of −OH and −NH−. Therefore, a new nanoprobe was designed by modifying the CDs with AAP. In this study, carboxyl-coated CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ethylenediamine and anhydrous citric acid. The introduction of ethylenediamine units can increase the Φ of CDs and the carboxyl groups in citric acid can promote dehydration and carbonation during the formation of CDs [20]. Besides, the abundant carboxyl groups on CDs surface were applied for further chemical modification with target molecular AAP through amide coupling reaction in the presence of coupling reagents EDC/NHS. The synthesis procedure of the proposed nanoprobe is shown in Scheme 1 .

Successful surface modification of AAP to CDs was investigated by TEM, FT-IR and XPS spectra. As indicated in Fig. 1 A and D, the TEM images revealed that the prepared CDs and CDs-AAP were well dispersed and exhibited uniform morphologies. Besides, High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of CDs and CDs-AAP showed a uniform well-resolved crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which were due to the (110) diffraction planes of sp2 carbon [28]. The size distribution diagrams are shown in Fig. 1C and F, the average particle size of CDs was 3.9 nm, whereas CDs-AAP was increased to 4.2 nm. The results confirm that the synthesis of CDs-AAP.

FT-IR spectra of AAP, CDs and CDs-AAP are shown in Fig. 2 A, a number of the primary absorption peaks can be assigned to the N−H and O−H stretching vibrations at around 3240 cm−1 and 3430 cm−1, the C−H and C=C stretching vibrations at 2892 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1, the peak at 1680 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 are associated to the stretching vibration of C=O and C−N of amide bond. Compare with the pristine CDs, the formation of amide bond between CDs and AAP reduces the absorption band of CDs-AAP at 3436 cm−1, and appears the N−H stretching vibration absorption band and bending vibration absorption band at 3249 cm−1 and 1742 cm−1, respectively. This demonstrated that AAP was covalently conjugated onto the surface of CDs through amide coupling reaction.

CDs-AAP were also corroborated by XPS study in Fig. 2B. The spectra with three peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV confirmed that CDs and CDs-AAP principally comprise carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements [29]. The high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of CDs and CDs-AAP are shown in Fig. S2. The inset in Fig. 2B are the elemental contents of CDs and CDs-AAP, which revealed that the N content and C/O atomic ratios of CDs were 11.96% and 2.68. After covalently conjugated with AAP, the N content and C/O atomic ratios of CDs-AAP were increased to 13.54% and 3.15, suggesting that AAP moieties were incorporated in CDs surface.

The synthesis of CDs-AAP was also analysis through Raman spectra and zeta potential. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, Raman spectra indicated that CDs and CDs-AAP both presence D band at around 1360 cm−1and G band at around 1578 cm−1 [30]. Besides, with increasing the amount of AAP on the surface of CDs, the ratio of ID/IG gradually increases from 0.46 to 0.77, which is attribute to the introduction of irregular sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the termination plane of disordered graphite. Moreover, the zeta potential of CDs and CDs-AAP were analyzed at pH 7.0 as shown in Fig. 3B, attribute to a number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the zeta potential of CDs is -16.13 mV. After conjugation with AAP, the zeta potential of CDs-AAP turns into -5.07 mV, which is ascribed to formation of amide bonds between carboxyl groups of CDs and amine group of AAP. Zata potential is consistent with Raman data analysis, which also prove that AAP was successfully coated onto CDs.

Considering the amount of AAP on CDs surface is responsible for OCl– detection, the amount of AAP on different polar nanoprobe was calculated based on the absorbance additivity method [24,25] (Figs. S3 and S4). The number of AAP on the small polarity of CDs-AAP was measured to be 16.60 mg g−1 and on the larger polarity was measured to be about 91.26 mg g−1. The Tyndall effect observed by laser in Fig. 4 further proved that different amount of AAP modified CDs were obtained, which was based on the fact that the larger particle size lead to the broader optical path. Furthermore, it also revealed that the proposed CDs-AAP exhibited good dispersibility in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs-AAP
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of prepared CDs and CD-AAP were also evaluated through FL and UV–vis absorption spectra. Fig. 5 are the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, they show red-shift emission peak from 442 nm to 456 nm when the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 nm to 400 nm. The optimum fluorescent emission is 448 nm when excited at 350 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs is measured to be 49.6% with quinine sulfate as reference standard. The inset in Fig. 5B are the fluorescence image of CDs dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0) under daylight and UV light. CDs exhibit a blue fluorescence signal, which is attributed to the intrinsic states in the highly crystalline structure.

Fig. 6 A shows the UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs, AAP and CDs-AAP, the characteristic absorption peak of CDs at 340 nm is due to the n-π* transition of C=O. AAP exhibits two absorption peaks at 245 and 275 nm, which can be assigned to the π-π* electronic transition of aromatic C=C and n-π* electronic transition of C=O [31]. After conjugation accomplished, CDs-AAP presence the corporate characteristic peaks of CDs and AAP. In addition, the absorption peak of CDs at 340 nm in CDs-AAP is red-shifted to 355 nm, this is because the modification increases the conjugated domain of CDs surface and forms new bonding orbitals and anti-bonds orbitals. Therefore, the energy difference of the transition level of π-π*and n-π* is reduced, leading to the absorption to the longwave direction. Fig. 6B represent the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-AAP as the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 nm to 400 nm. The optimum fluorescent emission is 484 nm when excited at 370 nm, which is in accordance with the condition used for in OCl– detection. Compared with CDs, CDs-AAP exhibit a cyan fluorescence signal (the inset in Fig. 6B). The emission wavelength is red-shifted as the effective conjugation length and surface functional groups increase, because of the reduced band gap due to π-electron delocalization in the sp2 domain [32,33]. In addition, the maximum emission of CDs-AAP at 484 nm is suitable for optical imaging of cells. The above results permit the use of CDs-AAP as fluorescent nanoprobes for cells imaging.

CCD was used for optimization of the detection conditions of pH, incubation time and temperature. 16 random experiments were selected to minimize the effect of uncontrolled variables (Table S2). The results acquired were then analyzed by analysis of variance according to Fisher’s statistical analysis. The relationship between the fluorescence response (F0–F) and the detection variables could be demonstrated by the follow equation:
(5)
F −
F
0
= + 889.3615 − 4.91439
X
1
+ 18.77957
X
2
+ 4.45477
X
3
+ 30.42977
X
1
X
2
+ 34.05457
X
1
X
3
+ 23.51061
X
2
X
3
− 1229.40681
X
1
2
− 66.09665
X
2
2
− 67.63181
X
3
2
Where X1, X2 and X3 are pH, incubation time and temperature, respectively. Table S3 was obtained from analysis of variance show the other statistical parameters and the reduced quadratic models of variables. The data in this table show that the P-value was less than 0.05 and F value was more than 0.05, implying significant model correlation between the variables and fluorescence responses. Furthermore, the R2 coefficient was 0.9955 and adjusted R2 was 0.9876, the consistency of R2 and adjusted R2 illustrates the established model was an efficient application model for predicting responses [34,35]. As can be seen from Fig. 7 A, it represents the relationship between model predicted values and actual data. Obviously, actual data is linearly distributed, indicating that there is sufficient consistency between the actual data and the data obtained from the model. Fig. 7B is the normal probability plots of the residuals. It shows that the residual usually falls on a straight line, suggesting that the error is normally distributed. In addition, the irregular residuals versus run number in Fig. 7C illustrates that the model was high efficiency for appropriate presentation of actual data. The above results demonstrated that the model can be used to navigate the design space.

Fig. 8 are the design-expert plots of variables and fluorescence response. They represent the interaction effect of the two variables on the fluorescence response for the curved surface. It can be seen that temperature, pH and incubation time demonstrated quadratic effects on the fluorescence response. Fig. 8A and B are the response surfaces for the influence of temperature. As the temperature increases, the quenching efficiency increases first and then decreases, with the optimum temperature is about 30 °C. Fig. 8B and C are the effect of incubation time of 5 – 40 min on fluorescence response, the optimum fluorescence response was obtained in incubation time of 20 min. Fig. 8A and C reveal the effect of OCl– on the quenching efficiency of CDs-AAP at different pH values, and the best detection pH is about 7.0. Overall, the optimum quenching efficiency is found to decrease when moving away from the red points on the curve, meaning that either increase or decrease in any of the detection variables will lead to decline of the fluorescence responses. The optimum sensing condition from the plots were obtained at pH = 7.0, incubation time 20 min and temperature 30 °C. Therefore, the obtained optimum sensing condition was chosen as the experimental conditions in the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CDs-AAP to OCl–
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the selectivity of CDs-AAP toward OCl–, the fluorescence responses of CDs-AAP to some coexistence anion including F−, S2O8 2−, Br−, Cl−, S2O3 2−, I−, NO2 −, SO4 2−, S2−, HSO3 −, Ac− and SO3 2− were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 9B, the fluorescence intensities of CDs-AAP and other anion in the absence and presence of OCl–, respectively. It was seen that a much higher relative fluorescence intensity was observed for CDs-AAP upon addition of other anion in the absence of OCl–. In contrast, a significant fluorescence decrease was observed by adding OCl– in this system. In addition, the selective experiments of CDs-AAP on cations and amino acids were also carried out as shown in Fig. S5, except that Cr6+ has a weak quenching effect on CDs-AAP, the other cations and amino acids have negligible effects on the fluorescence of CDs-AAP. These results demonstrate that CDs-AAP has better specificity to OCl−. Fig. 9C shows that the fluorescence of CDs-AAP can be quenched upon addition of 20 μM OCl– but other anions of the same concentration have negligible effect on UV light. Therefore, the influence from other anion was negligible in the OCl– detection, which demonstrates that the proposed CDs-AAP has excellent selectivity.

Based on the experimental results and the published conclusions [43–45], we envisaged that the strong oxidation capability of OCl– to AAP induced the process of the oxidization hydrolysis of hydrazide, then the Cl atom including partial positive charge (δ+) in OCl– can react with the recognition group of double bonds having partial negative charge (δ−) to produce chloride. Finally, the fluorescence of CDs-AAP was quenched by the heavy atom effect. This is because the formation of chloride increases the spin-orbit coupling in the fluorescent molecule, causing the singlet and triplet electrons of the excited state to be closer in energy (ie, the energy difference between the two is reduced), resulting in a decrease in the probability of generating fluorescence. To testify the detection mechanism of CDs-AAP to OCl–, the obtained reaction mixture of CDs-AAP and OCl– was characterized by XPS and FTIR. As present in Fig. 10 A, except the three peaks principally comprise carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements at 285, 400, and 532 eV, the addition of OCl– to CDs-AAP also presence the peak of Cl2p at 198.7 eV. The single peak of Cl2p is shown in the inset of Fig. 10A. The FTIR analysis in Fig. 10B are consistent with XPS spectra, apart from the primary absorption peaks of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations at around 3100 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1, the C=O and C–O stretching vibrations at 1584 cm−1 and 992 cm−1, the C–H deformation stretching vibration peak at 1350 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, it presence the C–Cl stretching vibrations at around 710 cm−1 [46,47], which successfully provided that the addition of OCl– to CDs-AAP solution can effectively form chloride and quench the fluorescence of CDs-AAP. Accordingly, the possible detection mechanism of CDs-AAP for tracking OCl– was described in Fig. 10C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of OCl– in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the reliability and applicability of the fluorescent nanoprobes in real water samples, CDs-AAP were used for detection of OCl– in tap water and pool water from the campus of Tianjin Polytechnic University. The real water samples were first filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane before using. Then the recovery studies were accomplished by spiking the samples containing various OCl– concentrations. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of OCl– in real water samples are in the range of 94.0% – 103% and 0.79 – 1.48, respectively. These results indicated that CDs-AAP has significant potential for quantitative detection of OCl– in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of OCl– in cells labeling and imaging
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are nano-fluorophores that possess the properties of nontoxicity, highly photostable and resistive to photobleaching. Besides, the size of CDs is similar with fluorescent proteins, therefore, they can be used as fluorescent tags for labeling living organisms [48]. To establish the application of CDs-AAP in cells labeling and imaging, the cytotoxicity of CDs-AAP with various concentrations for 48 h was assessed by MTT assays in VMSCs cells. Fig. 11 shows that even when the concentration of CDs-AAP reaches to 400 μg mL−1, a high cell viability of 82.1% is still observed, demonstrating the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of CDs-AAP prepared by our method.

Therefore, a cells label experiment was performed based on the bioconjugation interactions between the groups on surface of CDs-AAP and FimH lectin units in wild-type pili on the surface of E. coli. The fluorescence images of E. coli stained with CDs-AAP for 2 h as shown in Fig. 12 A, B and C. Its include the bright field images and fluorescence images under blue channel and green channel. The E. coli with bright fluorescent signals suggested that the cells internalization or endocytosis happened when the cells were incubated with CDs-AAP, which lead to bright blue and green fluorescent signals when observed under fluorescence microscope.

Considering that CDs-AAP can be efficiently internalized, they were introduced into VMSC cells for in vitro imaging. Fig. 12D, E and F are the confocal microscope images of VMSCs cells incubated with CDs-AAP for 6 h under bright field, blue channel and green channel. Significant fluorescence signals can be observed in the cell membrane and cytoplasmic regions, especially around the nucleus, implying that CDs-AAP can penetrate the cell membrane by cellular endocytosis. These results indicated that CDs-AAP synthesized in this work can be applied as a competent substitute for semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes for cells labeling and imaging.

In this study, a novel nanoprobe for quantitative detection of hypochlorite (OCl–) based on aminoantipyrine-functionalized carbon dots (CDs-AAP) were prepared through amide coupling reaction. The prepared CDs-AAP showed superior stability and high water dispersibility and solubility that were applied as a nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detecting OCl–. The optimal operating parameters during the detection process were assessed and optimized through central composite design (CCD). The detection limit is 40 nM in the linear range of 3 – 36 μM. This highly selective response to OCl– ensured CDs-AAP could be used to determine OCl– in environmental water samples. Furthermore, the low-cytotoxicity of CDs-AAP showed high fluorescence ability for labeling of E.coli and imaging VMSCs cells. The successful application of this nanoprobe system in cells imaging demonstrated that CDs-AAP have potential application in biological field. Moreover, the CDs-based nanoprobe design strategy can be extended to determine other analytes by changing the CDs with other specific recognition sites.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (18JCYBJC87500, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507) and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117543.
